claim: WHO states that fewer than half of health facilities in LMICs had uninterrupted access to medical oxygen and identifies production, distribution, power, equipment, funding, and maintenance as relevant system constraints.

source.title: Statement on access to quality and safe medical oxygen

method: Reviewed the WHO publication overview and linked technical document for the uninterrupted-access estimate and the listed system constraints.
limitations: The estimate is not a facility census and does not establish a common sampling frame across LMICs.
The statement does not quantify the marginal effect of any one constraint on patient outcomes.

claim: In a cross-sectional assessment of 2,884 facilities in 39 LMICs, reliable oxygen availability was reported by 24.5% of primary, 52.4% of secondary, and 66.8% of tertiary facilities.

source.title: A rapid facility-level assessment of oxygen systems in 39 low-income and middle-income countries: a cross-sectional study

method: Reviewed the open-access article abstract and methods summary, including facility sampling, three-month reliable-availability definition, and component availability measures.
limitations: Facility sampling was purposive and stratified rather than a globally representative census.
Reliability was reported by facility leadership for the preceding three months and is not equivalent to continuous patient-level delivery.

claim: WHO describes oxygen access as a system involving sources, distribution, regulation and conditioning, delivery, patient monitoring, power, and maintenance rather than a single equipment-presence indicator.

source.title: Health products policy and standards: Oxygen

method: Reviewed the WHO oxygen technical page sections on medical oxygen, system components, health-system levels, power, maintenance, and access challenges; no stable page update date was exposed.
limitations: The page is a living technical resource and its current access date is used because a publication date was not exposed.
The component framework does not itself provide comparable facility-level measurements.

claim: WHO and UNICEF provide harmonized specifications and maintenance guidance for oxygen-therapy devices intended for low- and middle-income health facilities and multiple decision-maker roles.

source.title: WHO-UNICEF Technical specifications and guidance for oxygen therapy devices

method: Reviewed the WHO publication overview for scope, intended users, procurement and maintenance content, and LMIC relevance.
limitations: Technical specifications do not show whether a facility has working equipment or uninterrupted supply.
Guidance is not an impact evaluation of procurement or maintenance interventions.

claim: WHO specifies minimum technical requirements for pressure-swing-adsorption plants used to produce medical-grade oxygen and states that high-quality medical-grade oxygen is required at all levels of care.

source.title: Technical specifications for Pressure Swing Adsorption(PSA) Oxygen Plants

method: Reviewed the WHO publication overview for the minimum-requirements statement and intended use of PSA plants as a medical-oxygen source.
limitations: The document addresses plant specifications, not downstream distribution, power continuity, maintenance, or patient delivery.
Meeting technical specifications does not establish that a plant is installed, functional, or adequately used.
